en.extract:
The grey penduline tit (Anthoscopus caroli), also known as the African penduline-tit, is a species of bird in the family Remizidae.  It is found in Angola, Botswana, Burundi, Republic of the Congo, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Eswatini, Kenya, Malawi, Mozambique, Namibia, Rwanda, South Africa, Tanzania, Uganda, Zambia, and Zimbabwe. Its natural habitats are subtropical or tropical dry forests, dry savanna, and moist savanna. At 8 to 9 cm (3.1 to 3.5 in) in length and a weight of 6.5 g (0.23 oz), it is one of the smallest species of bird found in Africa, along with its cousin the Cape penduline tit and the perhaps smaller mouse-colored penduline tit and the tit hylia.


Taxonomy
The grey penduline tit was formally described in 1871 by the English ornithologist Richard Bowdler Sharpe from a specimen that had been collected in the Ovaquenyama or Oukwanyama district of Damaraland. This is now   northern Namibia. Sharpe coined the binomial name Aegithalus caroli. This tit is now placed in the genus Anthoscopus that was introduced in 1851 by the German ornithologist Jean Cabanis. The genus name combines the Ancient Greek anthos meaning "blossom" or "flower" with skopos meaning "searcher". The specific epithet caroli is from Late Latin Carolus for "Charles". This was chosen to honour the Swedish explorer Karl Johan Andersson. 
Eleven subspecies are recognised:

A. c. roccatii Salvadori, 1906 Uganda, northeast DR Congo, Rwanda, Burundi, west Kenya and northwest Tanzania
A. c. pallescens Ulfstrand, 1960 – west Tanzania
A. c. ansorgei Hartert, E, 1905 – Congo, west DR Congo to central Angola
A. c. rhodesiae Sclater, WL, 1932 – southeast DR Congo, north Zambia and southwest Tanzania
A. c. robertsi Haagner, 1909 – southeast Kenya to ne Zambia, Malawi and north Mozambique
A. c. caroli (Sharpe, 1871) – south Angola and southwest Zambia to north Namibia and north Botswana
A. c. winterbottomi White, CMN, 1946 – south DR Congo and northwest Zambia
A. c. rankinei Irwin, 1963 – northeast Zimbabwe
A. c. hellmayri Roberts, 1914 – east, south Zimbabwe, south Mozambique and northeast South Africa
A. c. sylviella Reichenow, 1904 – south-central Kenya to central Tanzania
A. c. sharpei Hartert, E, 1905 – southwest Kenya to north Tanzania
The race A. c. sylviella, found in parts of Kenya and Tanzania, is sometimes regarded as a separate species, the buff-bellied penduline-tit.


References


External links
Grey Penduline Tit - Species text in The Atlas of Southern African Birds.

The '''grey penduline tit''' ('''''Anthoscopus caroli'''''), also known as the '''African penduline-tit''', is a species of bird in the family [[Remizidae]].  It is found in [[Angola]], [[Botswana]], [[Burundi]], [[Republic of the Congo]], [[Democratic Republic of the Congo]], [[Eswatini]], [[Kenya]], [[Malawi]], [[Mozambique]], [[Namibia]], [[Rwanda]], South Africa, [[Tanzania]], [[Uganda]], [[Zambia]], and [[Zimbabwe]]. Its natural [[habitat]]s are [[subtropical]] or tropical dry forests, dry [[savanna]], and moist savanna. At {{convert|8|to|9|cm|in|abbr=on}} in length and a weight of {{convert|6.5|g|oz|abbr=on}}, it is one of the smallest species of bird found in Africa, along with its cousin the [[Cape penduline tit]] and the perhaps smaller [[mouse-colored penduline tit]] and the [[tit hylia]].<ref>{{ cite book | last=Stevenson | first1=Terry | last2=Fanshawe | first2=John | year=2002 | title=Birds of East Africa | location=London | publisher=Christopher Helm | page=440 | isbn=978-0-7136-7347-0 }}</ref>

==Taxonomy==
The grey penduline tit was [[species description|formally described]] in 1871 by the English ornithologist [[Richard Bowdler Sharpe]] from a specimen that had been collected in the Ovaquenyama or [[Oukwanyama]] district of [[Damaraland]]. This is now   northern Namibia. Sharpe coined the [[binomial name]] ''Aegithalus caroli''.<ref>{{ cite journal | last1=Sharpe | first1=R. Bowdler | author1-link=Richard Bowdler Sharpe | date=1871 | title=On seven new or lately described species of African birds | journal=Ibis | series=3rd series |volume=1 | pages=414–417 [415–416] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/8587927 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Paynter | editor-first=Raymond A. Jr | year=1986 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=12 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | location=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=67 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14482184 }}</ref> This tit is now placed in the [[genus]] ''[[Anthoscopus]]'' that was introduced in 1851 by the German ornithologist [[Jean Cabanis]].<ref>{{ cite book | last=Cabanis | first=Jean | author-link=Jean Cabanis | year=1850–1851 | title=Museum Heineanum: Verzeichniss der ornithologischen Sammlung des Oberamtmann Ferdinand Heine, auf Gut St. Burchard vor Halberstadt | volume=1 | language=German, Latin | location=Halberstadt | publisher=R. Frantz | page=89 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/49584468 }}</ref><ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen | date=January 2022 | title=Waxwings and allies, tits, penduline tits | work=IOC World Bird List Version 12.1 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/waxwings/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=13 March 2022 }}</ref> The genus name combines the [[Ancient Greek]] ''anthos'' meaning "blossom" or "flower" with ''skopos'' meaning "searcher". The specific epithet ''caroli'' is from [[Late Latin]] ''Carolus'' for "Charles". This was chosen to honour the Swedish explorer [[Charles John Andersson|Karl Johan Andersson]].<ref>{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. | year=2010| title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | publisher=Christopher Helm | location=London | isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 | pages=[https://archive.org/stream/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling#page/n49/mode/1up 49], [https://archive.org/stream/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling#page/n91/mode/1up 91]}}</ref> 

Eleven [[subspecies]] are recognised:<ref name=ioc/>
* ''A. c. roccatii'' [[Tommaso Salvadori|Salvadori]], 1906 Uganda, northeast DR Congo, Rwanda, Burundi, west Kenya and northwest Tanzania
* ''A. c. pallescens'' Ulfstrand, 1960 – west Tanzania
* ''A. c. ansorgei'' [[Ernst Hartert|Hartert, E]], 1905 – Congo, west DR Congo to central Angola
* ''A. c. rhodesiae'' [[William Lutley Sclater|Sclater, WL]], 1932 – southeast DR Congo, north Zambia and southwest Tanzania
* ''A. c. robertsi'' [[Alwin Karl Haagner|Haagner]], 1909 – southeast Kenya to ne Zambia, Malawi and north Mozambique
* ''A. c. caroli'' ([[Richard Bowdler Sharpe|Sharpe]], 1871) – south Angola and southwest Zambia to north Namibia and north Botswana
* ''A. c. winterbottomi'' White, CMN, 1946 – south DR Congo and northwest Zambia
* ''A. c. rankinei'' [[Michael Patrick Stuart Irwin|Irwin]], 1963 – northeast Zimbabwe
* ''A. c. hellmayri'' [[Austin Roberts (zoologist)|Roberts]], 1914 – east, south Zimbabwe, south Mozambique and northeast South Africa
* ''A. c. sylviella'' [[Anton Reichenow|Reichenow]], 1904 – south-central Kenya to central Tanzania
* ''A. c. sharpei'' Hartert, E, 1905 – southwest Kenya to north Tanzania

The race ''A. c. sylviella'', found in parts of Kenya and Tanzania, is sometimes regarded as a separate species, the '''buff-bellied penduline-tit'''.
